subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The chapter systematically poses and solves linear equations to find unknowns — in the pyramid-filling problem (section 6.3: three equations solved for c = 20), the calendar magic trick (section 6.4: solving 4a + 16 = S for a), and the full set of Figure it Out word problems in section 6.6 (Q5–Q11: shrines, horses/hens, age, cowherds, dosa cart, Karim and the Genie). Equation-posing from contextual scenarios is the primary procedural strand throughout.

A distinct second strand runs through the chapter: using algebraic thinking to explain why tricks work and to invent new ones. Section 6.2 asks students to modify and design their own 'Think of a Number' tricks (E-4, E-5); section 6.3 asks students to generalise the pyramid-top formula to n rows and connect it to the Virahāṅka-Fibonacci sequence (E-14, E-15); section 6.5 uses algebraic expansion to prove the optimisation rule for the largest product; and section 6.6's divisibility proofs (E-23, E-24) require students to construct algebraic arguments, not just execute a procedure.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Section 6.5 and section 6.6 require students to produce deductive algebraic proofs — that qp × r is the largest product (by comparing expanded forms) and that the digit-reversal difference is always divisible by 9, 11, or 37 (E-23, E-24). These are not applications of a taught formula but formal justifications, directly matching the CG-6 criterion of algebra and elementary number theory.

The chapter explicitly frames algebra as a tool for puzzle-making and creative problem-solving. Students are invited to invent their own 'Think of a Number' trick (E-5), design their own calendar grid trick (E-17), and work through the open-ended Karim and the Genie scenario (E-32). These tasks require creative discovery, not retrieval of a learned solution.

Section 6.3 Figure it Out Q4–Q6 asks students to discover and articulate the rule connecting Virahāṅka-Fibonacci numbers to pyramid coefficients — exploring why placing Fibonacci numbers in the base row yields all Fibonacci numbers in the grid and a specific Fibonacci number at the top. This is substantive pattern-discovery grounded in the structure of the pyramid formula.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to pose, solve, and justify algebraic statements — using variable expressions and one-variable equations to decode tricks, prove divisibility rules, and solve word problems — by engaging both procedural equation-solving (C-2.4) and creative open-ended algebraic reasoning to explain and invent mathematical puzzles (C-2.5).
